input.prompt:
Design a detailed implementation plan for a bidirectional synchronization system in a Rails app. This is a take-home challenge deliverable (Crunchloop senior engineer challenge, Part 2). Do NOT write code — produce a concrete design plan with file paths, the reconciliation algorithm, schema changes, resilience strategy, and a test plan.

## Current project (Rails 7.0.4.3, Ruby, SQLite, RSpec)
Path: /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview

Local models:
- TodoList: `has_many :todo_items, dependent: :destroy`; `validates :name, presence: true`. Table `todo_lists` has ONLY a `name` column — NO created_at/updated_at timestamps.
- TodoItem: `belongs_to :todo_list`; `validates :title, presence: true`; `validates :complete, inclusion: {in:[true,false]}`. Table `todo_items` has: title (string), complete (boolean default false), todo_list_id (fk), created_at, updated_at.

Gems present: rails 7.0.4.3, sqlite3, puma, turbo-rails, stimulus-rails, tailwindcss-rails, importmap-rails, jbuilder, rspec-rails, capybara, selenium-webdriver, webdrivers. NO http client, NO background job backend, NO WebMock/VCR yet.
Existing RSpec setup at spec/ (models, requests, routing, controllers/api). There's an existing `Api::TodoListsController#index` returning JSON via jbuilder, and a `.devcontainer/` (Ruby 3.1 image, postCreate runs bundle install + rake db:setup).

## External API (the system to sync with) — from its OpenAPI spec
Base URL configurable (docs say http://localhost), no auth. IDs are STRINGS. Models have a `source_id` field (string) meant to hold the originating system's id.
Endpoints:
- GET /todolists  -> returns all lists WITH nested items
- POST /todolists -> create list, body {source_id, name, items:[{source_id, description, completed}]} (nested item creation supported in ONE call)
- PATCH /todolists/{id} -> body {name}
- DELETE /todolists/{id}
- PATCH /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId} -> body {description, completed}
- DELETE /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}
External TodoList fields: id(string), source_id(string), name, created_at, updated_at, items[]. External TodoItem fields: id(string), source_id(string), description(boolean? no: string), completed(boolean), created_at, updated_at.
FIELD MAPPING: local `title` <-> external `description`; local `complete` <-> external `completed`. Note there's no item-create or item-only POST endpoint other than nesting in the list POST — so creating a NEW item on an EXISTING external list is a gap to address in the design (options: recreate strategy, or note as limitation; think it through).

## Confirmed decisions from the user
1. Scope: BIDIRECTIONAL reconciliation. Push local->external (create/update/delete) AND pull external->local (create/update/delete, including creating local TodoLists/TodoItems when detected in external). Conflict resolution: last-write-wins using updated_at.
2. Testing: mock the external HTTP in tests (WebMock and/or VCR). The sync targets a base URL configurable via ENV. Do not depend on a live external service.
3. Execution: UPGRADE Rails 7.0.4.3 -> 7.1.x and add Solid Queue. The sync core must be a plain Ruby service object (PORO) that is fully unit-testable independent of the queue. It should be invocable BOTH by a rake task (the runnable POC) AND wrapped in an ActiveJob enqueued via Solid Queue. Retries: combine ActiveJob retry_on for transient whole-job failures with per-record retry/backoff inside the service so partial failures don't redo already-synced records.

## What I need from you
1. Schema/migration design: what columns to add to map local<->external and to track sync state and deletions. Consider: external_id (string) on both tables, last_synced_at, ADDING timestamps to todo_lists (currently missing!), and how to propagate LOCAL deletes to the external side (a hard delete leaves no trace — propose soft-delete vs a tombstone/sync-log table, with trade-offs). Recommend one.
2. The reconciliation algorithm in detail: how to match records (external_id, fallback to source_id), and the decision matrix for each case: exists-local-only, exists-external-only, exists-both. For each, create/update/delete decisions in both directions, and how updated_at drives last-write-wins. Handle the nested items within each list.
3. Performance: how to minimize external calls (single GET /todolists pulls everything with items; batch create via nested POST; only PATCH when changed/dirty; etc.).
4. Resilience: partial-failure handling, retry with backoff, idempotency (re-running sync must be safe), and logging strategy.
5. File/module layout: service objects (e.g. app/services/external_todo_api/client.rb, app/sync/...), the ActiveJob, the rake task, config for ENV base URL.
6. Rails 7.0->7.1 upgrade steps + Solid Queue install steps (gem, install generator, migrations, config, the worker process, devcontainer integration) — keep it minimal and low-risk.
7. HTTP client choice (Faraday vs Net::HTTP) with a recommendation suitable for a clean challenge submission.
8. Test plan: unit tests for the reconciler (all matrix cases), client tests with WebMock, and an integration-style sync test. What to assert.
9. NOTES.md outline: the sections to include.
10. Edge cases and risks to call out.

Read the actual files to ground your plan (models, schema.rb, config/routes.rb, the existing Api controller and jbuilder views, .devcontainer/, Gemfile, spec/rails_helper.rb). Return a thorough, well-structured plan I can hand to an implementer.

content.text:
I now have a complete picture. Here is the full implementation plan.

---

# Bidirectional Sync Implementation Plan — Crunchloop Rails Challenge Part 2

## 0. Context grounded in the actual repo

- `db/schema.rb` confirms `todo_lists` has **only** `name` (no `id`-aside timestamps), `todo_items` has `title`, `complete` (default false), `todo_list_id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`.
- Models at `app/models/todo_list.rb` / `todo_item.rb` are minimal; `TodoList has_many :todo_items, dependent: :destroy`.
- The existing API namespace pattern (`app/controllers/api/todo_lists_controller.rb`, jbuilder at `app/views/api/todo_lists/index.json.jbuilder`) shows the local app *exposes* an outbound API too — the external `source_id` field is designed to hold *our* local id when the external system pulls from us. This matters: when we POST to the external system, we set `source_id` = our local record id, which gives us a stable correlation key even before we learn the external `id`.
- `app/controllers/application_controller.rb` raises on unknown formats — unrelated to sync but confirms conventions.
- RSpec is configured with transactional fixtures (`spec/rails_helper.rb`); no `spec/support` autoload yet (the `Dir[...support...]` line is commented). We'll need to uncomment/add support wiring for WebMock.
- `Procfile.dev` exists (`web` + `css`) — we add a `worker` line.
- Devcontainer: Ruby 3.1, `postCreateCommand` runs `bundle install && rake db:setup`.

---

## 1. Schema / migration design

Four migrations, each small and reversible. Keep `external_id`/`source_id` as STRINGS (external IDs are strings).

### Migration A — add timestamps to `todo_lists` (currently missing)
```
add_timestamps :todo_lists, null: true   # backfill, then change to null: false
```
Two-step within one migration: `add_timestamps` with `null: true`, backfill existing rows to `current_timestamp`, then `change_column_null` to `false`. This is required because last-write-wins needs a local `updated_at` on lists, and lists currently have none. This is the single most important schema gap.

### Migration B — sync columns on `todo_lists`
- `external_id` (string, indexed, unique where not null) — the external system's `id`.
- `last_synced_at` (datetime, nullable) — when this record was last successfully reconciled.

### Migration C — sync columns on `todo_items`
- `external_id` (string, indexed, unique where not null).
- `last_synced_at` (datetime, nullable).

### Migration D — deletion tracking (the design decision)

**The problem:** a local hard delete (`dependent: :destroy`) leaves no row, so the next sync can't tell "deleted locally, must DELETE on external" apart from "never existed locally, must PULL-create from external." Without a trace, a delete you made locally would be *resurrected* by the next pull.

**Options considered:**

| Option | How | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soft delete (`deleted_at` column + default scope) | Records never physically removed; `deleted_at` set instead | Full history; simple to reason about; one place | Forces `default_scope`/`unscope` everywhere; changes existing app + existing specs (`destroys its items when destroyed` test); leaks sync concern into the whole domain |
| **Tombstone / sync-log table** | Separate `sync_tombstones(record_type, record_id, external_id, deleted_at)` written via model callbacks on destroy | Domain models untouched; existing destroy semantics & specs keep passing; explicit, sync-owned; easy to purge after propagation | One extra table + callback; must remember to write it |
| Do nothing | — | Trivial | Deletes get resurrected — correctness bug |

**Recommendation: the tombstone table.** It keeps the existing app and its passing specs (notably the `dependent: :destroy` test) completely intact, and isolates the sync concern. For a challenge submission this reads as a deliberate, well-justified design decision rather than an invasive `default_scope` refactor.

```
create_table :sync_tombstones do |t|
  t.string   :record_type, null: false   # "TodoList" | "TodoItem"
  t.bigint   :record_id,   null: false   # local id (for traceability)
  t.string   :external_id                # null if record was never pushed
  t.bigint   :parent_external_id_ref     # for items: the list's external id, needed for DELETE /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}
  t.datetime :deleted_at,  null: false
  t.datetime :propagated_at              # set once the external DELETE succeeds
  t.timestamps
end
add_index :sync_tombstones, [:record_type, :external_id]
add_index :sync_tombstones, :propagated_at
```

A tombstone is only actionable if it has an `external_id` (the record existed remotely). After a successful external DELETE we set `propagated_at` (idempotency — never delete twice). Old propagated tombstones can be purged periodically.

**Model wiring** (concern `app/models/concerns/syncable.rb` or `after_destroy` hooks): on destroy of a `TodoList`/`TodoItem` that has an `external_id`, create a tombstone capturing `external_id` (and for items, the parent list's `external_id`). For items destroyed via `dependent: :destroy` cascade when the *whole list* is deleted, we typically only need the list-level DELETE (external cascades), so we can skip per-item tombstones in that path to avoid noise — but we still need the item-level tombstone when a single item is deleted while its list survives.

---

## 2. Reconciliation algorithm

### Matching strategy (correlation keys, in priority order)
1. **`external_id`** on the local record ↔ external `id`. Primary key once a record has been synced.
2. **Fallback: `source_id`** — when we POST a new local record to the external system we set `source_id` = local id. So an external record whose `source_id` equals a local record's id is the same record even if we never persisted its `external_id` (e.g., the push succeeded but the local `external_id` write failed — a crash window). On pull we use this to repair the link instead of creating a duplicate.
3. No match either way → genuinely one-sided.

Build two indexes per sync run for O(1) matching:
- `local_by_external_id`, `local_by_id` (for source_id fallback).
- `external_by_id`, `external_by_source_id`.

### Decision matrix — TodoLists (top level)

Let `L` = local record, `R` = external (remote) record. `last-write-wins` compares `L.updated_at` vs `R.updated_at` (both parsed to UTC; external timestamps come as ISO strings).

| Case | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Local only, never pushed** | `L.external_id` nil, no `R` matches by source_id | PUSH create: `POST /todolists` with `source_id=L.id, name, items:[...]` (nested). Store returned `external_id` on `L` and on each item. Set `last_synced_at`. |
| **External only, no local** | `R` exists, no `L` by external_id and no `L.id == R.source_id` | PULL create: build local `TodoList` (+ nested items) with `external_id=R.id`. Set `last_synced_at`. |
| **Both exist** | matched | Compare timestamps → see update rules below; then recurse into items. |
| **Tombstone (local deleted)** | actionable tombstone with `external_id` and nil `propagated_at` | PUSH delete: `DELETE /todolists/{external_id}`; on success set `propagated_at`. |
| **External deleted** | `L.external_id` present + `L.last_synced_at` present, but `R` absent from the full GET | PULL delete: destroy `L` locally. (Guard: only when `L` was previously synced — a brand-new unsynced local list also has no `R`, but it's caught by the "local only, never pushed" case because its `external_id` is nil.) |

**Update rule (both exist):**
- If `L.updated_at > R.updated_at` and the mapped fields differ → PATCH external (`PATCH /todolists/{id}` body `{name}`).
- If `R.updated_at > L.updated_at` and fields differ → update `L` locally (`name`), **without bumping `updated_at` past R** (use `update_columns` or set `updated_at = R.updated_at` so we don't create a false "local is newer" on the next run).
- If equal or no field diff → no-op (dirty check minimizes calls).
- Always recurse into items.

### Decision matrix — TodoItems (nested within a matched list)

Match items within the list scope by `external_id`, fallback `source_id`. Field mapping: local `title` ↔ external `description`; local `complete` ↔ external `completed`.

| Case | Action |
|---|---|
| Local item, no external_id, list NOT yet on external | Handled by the parent's nested `POST` (items go in the create body). |
| Local item, no external_id, **list already exists on external** | **The API gap** (no item-create endpoint). See strategy below. |
| External item, no local match | PULL create local `TodoItem(external_id=R.id, title=R.description, complete=R.completed)`. |
| Both exist | Last-write-wins: if local newer & differs → `PATCH /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}` body `{description, completed}`; if external newer & differs → update local columns; else no-op. |
| Local item tombstone w/ external_id | `DELETE /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}`; set `propagated_at`. |
| External item missing but local was synced | PULL delete: destroy local item. |

### The "new item on an existing external list" gap

The external API only supports item creation nested in `POST /todolists`. Three strategies, with a recommendation:

1. **Recreate-list strategy:** DELETE the external list and re-POST it with all items. Correct but destructive — loses external item IDs/history and risks data loss if the external side had its own changes. Reject.
2. **Accept-as-limitation + document:** push new items only at list-creation time; for new items added to an already-synced list, log a warning and skip (or pull-only). Honest, low-risk.
3. **Recommended — "best effort with documented limitation":** Attempt nothing destructive. In `push` for an existing list, if there are local items with no `external_id`, log them clearly and record a non-fatal `SyncResult` warning. Document prominently in NOTES.md that the external contract lacks an item-create endpoint, so newly-created local items on an existing list cannot be pushed without a list recreate, which we deliberately avoid. This demonstrates you understood the contract constraint and made a safe call. (Mention recreate as the alternative if the product accepted destructive sync.)

### Last-write-wins precision note

External lists have `created_at/updated_at`; after Migration A, local lists do too. Compare in UTC. Define a tolerance: if the absolute difference is within a small epsilon (e.g., 1s) treat as equal/no-op to avoid ping-pong from clock skew and serialization rounding.

---

## 3. Performance — minimize external calls

- **One GET pulls everything:** `GET /todolists` returns all lists *with nested items*. A single call gives the entire remote snapshot — no per-list or per-item GETs.
- **Batch create via nested POST:** new local lists send all their items in one `POST` body.
- **Dirty checks before PATCH:** only PATCH when the mapped fields actually differ AND the side is the newer writer. Never blind-write.
- **In-memory diff:** build the two index hashes once, do all matching in Ruby; the only network calls are the single GET plus the necessary mutations.
- **Skip clean records:** records where `last_synced_at >= updated_at` on both sides and present in the snapshot need no write.
- Optional future optimization (note only): if the external API later supports `If-Modified-Since` or a `?since=` filter, switch from full-snapshot to delta pull.

---

## 4. Resilience

- **Partial failure isolation:** the reconciler processes records independently. A failure on one list/item is caught, recorded in a `SyncResult` (per-record status: created/updated/deleted/skipped/failed + error), and the loop continues. One bad record never aborts the run.
- **Per-record retry/backoff inside the service:** wrap each external mutation in a small retry helper (e.g., 3 attempts, exponential backoff 0.5s/1s/2s) for transient errors (timeouts, 5xx, connection reset). 4xx (except 429) are non-retryable and recorded as failures immediately.
- **Idempotency (re-run safety):**
  - `source_id` + `external_id` correlation means a re-run never double-creates: a record pushed but whose local `external_id` write was lost is re-matched by `source_id` on the next GET and the link is repaired rather than re-created.
  - Tombstones with `propagated_at` are never re-deleted.
  - `last_synced_at` + dirty checks make redundant PATCHes no-ops.
  - The whole run is convergent: running it twice with no changes performs zero mutations the second time.
- **Whole-job retry via ActiveJob:** `retry_on` for transient infrastructure failures (e.g., the external host briefly down) so the *job* retries; because the service is idempotent, a job retry safely re-reconciles only what's still pending. `discard_on` for permanent errors (e.g., malformed config).
- **Transaction boundaries:** wrap *local* writes for a single record (list + its items, or item) in a DB transaction so a local-side failure doesn't leave a half-linked record. Do NOT wrap the whole run in one transaction (you'd roll back already-confirmed external mutations).
- **Logging:** structured, tagged logger (`Rails.logger.tagged("Sync")`), one line per decision (`PUSH_CREATE list source_id=…`, `PATCH item external_id=… fields=[completed]`, `SKIP clean`, `FAIL … error=…`). Emit a summary at the end (counts per action + failures). Return the `SyncResult` so the rake task can print it and tests can assert on it.

---

## 5. File / module layout

```
app/
  services/
    external_todo_api/
      client.rb            # HTTP wrapper: list_todolists, create_todolist, update_todolist,
                           #   delete_todolist, update_todoitem, delete_todoitem
      configuration.rb     # base_url from ENV, timeouts, Faraday connection
      resource_error.rb    # raised on non-2xx, carries status + body; flags retryable?
  sync/
    todo_sync_service.rb   # PORO orchestrator: pull snapshot, run reconciler, apply, return SyncResult
    reconciler.rb          # pure-ish diff engine: given local state + remote snapshot -> list of Actions
    list_reconciler.rb     # per-list matching incl. nested item reconciliation (optional split)
    action.rb             # value object: {kind, target, payload}
    sync_result.rb         # accumulates per-record outcomes + summary
    mapper.rb              # field mapping local<->external (title<->description, complete<->completed)
  models/
    concerns/
      syncable.rb          # after_destroy -> create tombstone (when external_id present)
    sync_tombstone.rb      # tombstone model
  jobs/
    todo_sync_job.rb       # ActiveJob: retry_on/discard_on, calls TodoSyncService.new(...).call

lib/
  tasks/
    sync.rake              # `rake sync:run` (the runnable POC) -> TodoSyncService.new.call; prints result

config/
  initializers/
    external_todo_api.rb   # reads ENV["EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL"] (default http://localhost:3001 or similar)
  # queue_adapter set to solid_queue in config/environments/*

spec/
  support/webmock.rb       # require 'webmock/rspec'; WebMock.disable_net_connect!
  services/external_todo_api/client_spec.rb
  sync/reconciler_spec.rb
  sync/todo_sync_service_spec.rb        # integration-style with WebMock
  jobs/todo_sync_job_spec.rb
  models/sync_tombstone_spec.rb
  fixtures/ or spec/support/api_responses/  # canned JSON for stubs
```

**Separation that makes it testable:** `Reconciler` is pure — it takes the local records and the parsed remote snapshot and returns a list of `Action`s with **no I/O**. `TodoSyncService` does the I/O (calls `Client.list_todolists`, runs the reconciler, applies actions through the client + ActiveRecord). This lets you unit-test the entire decision matrix without HTTP at all, and reserve WebMock for the client and the integration test.

**Config:** `EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL` env var, read in an initializer into `ExternalTodoApi::Configuration`. The client reads from config so tests can point it at a stub host.

---

## 6. Rails 7.0 → 7.1 + Solid Queue (minimal, low-risk)

### Rails upgrade
1. Gemfile: `gem "rails", "~> 7.1.0"` (and bump `puma` to `~> 6.0`, which 7.1 apps expect; 5.x works but 6 is cleaner). `bundle update rails`.
2. Run `bin/rails app:update` — review/accept new framework defaults files; **do not** blindly flip `config.load_defaults 7.1` until verified. Safer path: keep `load_defaults 7.0`, then create `config/initializers/new_framework_defaults_7_1.rb` and opt into changes incrementally. For a clean submission, flipping to `7.1` and running the suite is fine since the app is tiny — but call out the deprecations you resolved in NOTES.md.
3. The stdlib gem shims currently pinned in the Gemfile (`logger`, `base64`, `bigdecimal`, `mutex_m`, `drb`, `benchmark`, `ostruct`, `cgi`) were added for Ruby/Rails 7.0 compatibility; with 7.1 + Ruby 3.1 most are still harmless to keep. Leave them to minimize risk.
4. `bundle exec rspec` — fix any deprecations. Regenerate `db/schema.rb` header to `[7.1]` via a migration run.

### Solid Queue install
1. Gemfile: `gem "solid_queue"`. `bundle install`.
2. `bin/rails solid_queue:install` (generates the migration for the queue tables + a `config/queue.yml` or initializer depending on version). On 7.1, Solid Queue runs on its own tables in the **same SQLite DB** for the challenge (a separate `queue` database via multi-db is the production-grade option — note it but keep single-DB for simplicity).
3. Run the generated migration: adds `solid_queue_*` tables.
4. Config: in `config/environments/development.rb` and `production.rb`: `config.active_job.queue_adapter = :solid_queue`. For **test**: `config.active_job.queue_adapter = :test` (so `TodoSyncJob` enqueues can be asserted without a running worker).
5. Worker process: add to `Procfile.dev`:
   ```
   worker: bin/rails solid_queue:start
   ```
   (or `bin/jobs` on newer Solid Queue). Document running it for the queued path; the rake task path needs no worker.
6. Devcontainer: no change strictly required since `postCreateCommand` runs `bundle install && rake db:setup`, and `db:setup` will load the new schema including queue tables. Optionally note that the worker must be started manually (`bin/rails solid_queue:start`) or add it to a documented startup step. Keep `postCreateCommand` as-is to stay low-risk.

---

## 7. HTTP client: Faraday vs Net::HTTP

**Recommendation: Faraday.** Rationale for a clean challenge submission:
- Clean, declarative connection setup (base_url, JSON request/response middleware, timeouts) — less boilerplate than hand-rolling `Net::HTTP` request objects and JSON parsing.
- First-class with WebMock and VCR (the testing requirement), which everyone recognizes.
- Pluggable retry middleware (`faraday-retry`) complements the in-service retry, and easy error-handling via `response.success?` / `raise_error` middleware.
- It's the de-facto standard for Ruby API clients, so reviewers read it instantly.

Trade-off: one extra dependency vs. zero-dep `Net::HTTP`. For a *library-grade* sync client the readability win outweighs the dependency. Add `gem "faraday"` (and optionally `gem "faraday-retry"`). If you wanted zero new runtime deps, `Net::HTTP` + `JSON` is viable and I'd wrap it behind the same `Client` interface — but Faraday is the better submission choice.

---

## 8. Test plan

### Reconciler unit tests (`spec/sync/reconciler_spec.rb`) — pure, no HTTP
Feed in local records (built/created) + a parsed remote snapshot hash, assert the returned `Action` list. Cover the full matrix:
- local-only list → expect a `push_create` action with nested items + `source_id`.
- external-only list → expect `pull_create` with mapped fields.
- both, local newer & different → `push_update`.
- both, external newer & different → `pull_update`.
- both, equal/no diff → no action (clean skip).
- tombstone with external_id → `push_delete`.
- previously-synced local list absent from snapshot → `pull_delete`.
- item-level variants of each within a matched list (incl. field mapping `title↔description`, `complete↔completed`).
- the API-gap case: new item on existing external list → expect a warning action / skip, not a destructive recreate.
- timestamp tie within epsilon → no action.
- `source_id` fallback: external record whose `source_id` == local id but local `external_id` nil → expect link-repair, not duplicate create.

### Client tests with WebMock (`spec/services/external_todo_api/client_spec.rb`)
- `list_todolists` → stub `GET /todolists` returning canned nested JSON; assert parsed structure.
- `create_todolist` → assert request body contains `source_id`, `name`, nested `items` with `description`/`completed`; stub 201 with returned `id`s; assert client returns them.
- `update_todolist` / `update_todoitem` → assert PATCH path + body shape.
- `delete_*` → assert DELETE path; assert 204 handled.
- Error handling: stub 500 → raises retryable `ResourceError`; stub 422 → raises non-retryable; stub timeout (`to_timeout`) → retryable. Assert `disable_net_connect!` is active (no real calls leak).

### Integration-style sync test (`spec/sync/todo_sync_service_spec.rb`)
- Seed local DB + stub the full external surface with WebMock. Run `TodoSyncService.new.call`.
- Assert: correct external requests were made (WebMock `have_requested` with body matchers), local DB ends in the expected converged state (new pulled lists/items exist with `external_id` set, `last_synced_at` set, tombstones marked `propagated_at`).
- **Idempotency assertion:** run `.call` a second time with the snapshot updated to reflect the first run; assert **zero** mutating requests the second time.
- **Partial-failure assertion:** stub one PATCH to 500; assert the run completes, that record is marked failed in `SyncResult`, and the other records still synced.

### Job test (`spec/jobs/todo_sync_job_spec.rb`)
- With `:test` adapter, assert `TodoSyncJob.perform_later` enqueues; `perform_now` invokes `TodoSyncService`. Assert `retry_on` is configured (e.g., it re-enqueues on the transient error class). Keep this thin — the heavy logic is tested in the service.

### Tombstone model test
- Destroying a synced list/item creates a tombstone with `external_id`; destroying an unsynced record does not.

### Test infra
- Add `gem "webmock"` (and optionally `vcr`) to `:test`. Recommend **WebMock** primarily (explicit, readable stubs); VCR optional for the integration test if you want recorded cassettes — but WebMock alone satisfies the requirement and keeps the repo free of cassette files. Create `spec/support/webmock.rb` and uncomment the `spec/support` autoload line in `spec/rails_helper.rb`.

---

## 9. NOTES.md outline

1. **Overview** — what the sync does (bidirectional, last-write-wins).
2. **How to run** — `EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL=... bundle exec rake sync:run` (POC); queued path via `TodoSyncJob.perform_later` + starting the Solid Queue worker.
3. **Architecture** — Client / Reconciler (pure) / Service / Job / Rake separation and why.
4. **Data model & migrations** — new columns, the tombstone table, and the timestamps-added-to-todo_lists fix.
5. **Reconciliation algorithm** — matching keys (external_id → source_id fallback), the decision matrix, last-write-wins with epsilon.
6. **Field mapping** — `title↔description`, `complete↔completed`, `source_id` semantics.
7. **Resilience & idempotency** — per-record retry, ActiveJob retry, re-run safety, transactions, logging.
8. **Performance** — single-GET snapshot, nested batch create, dirty-check PATCH.
9. **Known limitations / trade-offs** — the **item-create API gap** (no endpoint to add an item to an existing external list; recreate avoided deliberately); single SQLite DB shared with Solid Queue; full-snapshot pull (no delta); last-write-wins drops the losing edit (no field-level merge).
10. **Decisions log** — tombstone vs soft-delete, Faraday vs Net::HTTP, Rails 7.1 + Solid Queue.
11. **Testing** — how the suite is structured and how to run it.
12. **Future work** — delta sync, separate queue DB, conflict audit trail, webhook-driven sync.

---

## 10. Edge cases & risks

- **`todo_lists` has no timestamps today** — last-write-wins is impossible for lists until Migration A lands. Highest-priority fix; backfill carefully so existing rows don't all look "ancient."
- **Crash between external POST and local `external_id` write** — handled by `source_id` fallback on re-run (link repair, no duplicate).
- **Delete resurrection** — without tombstones a local delete is undone by the next pull; tombstones + `propagated_at` prevent this and prevent double-deletes.
- **Clock skew / timestamp rounding** between systems → ping-pong updates. Mitigated by the epsilon tolerance and by setting local `updated_at` to the remote value on pull-update.
- **External `description`/`completed` nil or type surprises** (the spec was ambiguous on item field types) — mapper must coerce `completed` to boolean and validate `description` presence to satisfy the local `title` presence validation; record a failure rather than crash if invalid.
- **Validation failures on pull-create** (e.g., empty external name) — wrap in transaction, record failure, continue.
- **Item-create gap** — new local items on an existing external list can't be pushed without a destructive recreate; documented as a limitation.
- **`dependent: :destroy` cascade vs item tombstones** — deleting a whole list shouldn't spam per-item tombstones (external cascades on list DELETE); only single-item deletes need item tombstones. Get this guard right or you'll issue DELETEs for items the list-delete already removed.
- **No auth / no pagination in the external API** — fine for the challenge; note that a real system needs both (and pagination would break the single-GET assumption).
- **Rails 7.1 `load_defaults` flip** could surface deprecations in the existing app; verify the full suite, and the existing `Api::TodoListsController` format-error behavior, still pass.
- **Solid Queue on SQLite** — works for the POC but has concurrency limits; acceptable, noted as a trade-off.
- **Concurrent sync runs** — two overlapping runs could double-act. Add a simple guard (advisory lock / a "sync in progress" flag) or document that runs are expected to be serialized by the single worker.

### Critical Files for Implementation
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/db/schema.rb (and new files under /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/db/migrate/)
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/app/models/todo_list.rb and /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/app/models/todo_item.rb
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/Gemfile
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/spec/rails_helper.rb
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/config/application.rb (and config/environments/* for the Solid Queue adapter)

Note on process: I was given conflicting instructions (a read-only planning role with no edit tools, vs. plan mode offering a plan file). I treated this as read-only and returned the full plan here rather than writing any file. If you'd like, I can hand this off to an implementer or you can switch me out of plan mode to execute it.

toolUseResult.prompt:
Design a detailed implementation plan for a bidirectional synchronization system in a Rails app. This is a take-home challenge deliverable (Crunchloop senior engineer challenge, Part 2). Do NOT write code — produce a concrete design plan with file paths, the reconciliation algorithm, schema changes, resilience strategy, and a test plan.

## Current project (Rails 7.0.4.3, Ruby, SQLite, RSpec)
Path: /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview

Local models:
- TodoList: `has_many :todo_items, dependent: :destroy`; `validates :name, presence: true`. Table `todo_lists` has ONLY a `name` column — NO created_at/updated_at timestamps.
- TodoItem: `belongs_to :todo_list`; `validates :title, presence: true`; `validates :complete, inclusion: {in:[true,false]}`. Table `todo_items` has: title (string), complete (boolean default false), todo_list_id (fk), created_at, updated_at.

Gems present: rails 7.0.4.3, sqlite3, puma, turbo-rails, stimulus-rails, tailwindcss-rails, importmap-rails, jbuilder, rspec-rails, capybara, selenium-webdriver, webdrivers. NO http client, NO background job backend, NO WebMock/VCR yet.
Existing RSpec setup at spec/ (models, requests, routing, controllers/api). There's an existing `Api::TodoListsController#index` returning JSON via jbuilder, and a `.devcontainer/` (Ruby 3.1 image, postCreate runs bundle install + rake db:setup).

## External API (the system to sync with) — from its OpenAPI spec
Base URL configurable (docs say http://localhost), no auth. IDs are STRINGS. Models have a `source_id` field (string) meant to hold the originating system's id.
Endpoints:
- GET /todolists  -> returns all lists WITH nested items
- POST /todolists -> create list, body {source_id, name, items:[{source_id, description, completed}]} (nested item creation supported in ONE call)
- PATCH /todolists/{id} -> body {name}
- DELETE /todolists/{id}
- PATCH /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId} -> body {description, completed}
- DELETE /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}
External TodoList fields: id(string), source_id(string), name, created_at, updated_at, items[]. External TodoItem fields: id(string), source_id(string), description(boolean? no: string), completed(boolean), created_at, updated_at.
FIELD MAPPING: local `title` <-> external `description`; local `complete` <-> external `completed`. Note there's no item-create or item-only POST endpoint other than nesting in the list POST — so creating a NEW item on an EXISTING external list is a gap to address in the design (options: recreate strategy, or note as limitation; think it through).

## Confirmed decisions from the user
1. Scope: BIDIRECTIONAL reconciliation. Push local->external (create/update/delete) AND pull external->local (create/update/delete, including creating local TodoLists/TodoItems when detected in external). Conflict resolution: last-write-wins using updated_at.
2. Testing: mock the external HTTP in tests (WebMock and/or VCR). The sync targets a base URL configurable via ENV. Do not depend on a live external service.
3. Execution: UPGRADE Rails 7.0.4.3 -> 7.1.x and add Solid Queue. The sync core must be a plain Ruby service object (PORO) that is fully unit-testable independent of the queue. It should be invocable BOTH by a rake task (the runnable POC) AND wrapped in an ActiveJob enqueued via Solid Queue. Retries: combine ActiveJob retry_on for transient whole-job failures with per-record retry/backoff inside the service so partial failures don't redo already-synced records.

## What I need from you
1. Schema/migration design: what columns to add to map local<->external and to track sync state and deletions. Consider: external_id (string) on both tables, last_synced_at, ADDING timestamps to todo_lists (currently missing!), and how to propagate LOCAL deletes to the external side (a hard delete leaves no trace — propose soft-delete vs a tombstone/sync-log table, with trade-offs). Recommend one.
2. The reconciliation algorithm in detail: how to match records (external_id, fallback to source_id), and the decision matrix for each case: exists-local-only, exists-external-only, exists-both. For each, create/update/delete decisions in both directions, and how updated_at drives last-write-wins. Handle the nested items within each list.
3. Performance: how to minimize external calls (single GET /todolists pulls everything with items; batch create via nested POST; only PATCH when changed/dirty; etc.).
4. Resilience: partial-failure handling, retry with backoff, idempotency (re-running sync must be safe), and logging strategy.
5. File/module layout: service objects (e.g. app/services/external_todo_api/client.rb, app/sync/...), the ActiveJob, the rake task, config for ENV base URL.
6. Rails 7.0->7.1 upgrade steps + Solid Queue install steps (gem, install generator, migrations, config, the worker process, devcontainer integration) — keep it minimal and low-risk.
7. HTTP client choice (Faraday vs Net::HTTP) with a recommendation suitable for a clean challenge submission.
8. Test plan: unit tests for the reconciler (all matrix cases), client tests with WebMock, and an integration-style sync test. What to assert.
9. NOTES.md outline: the sections to include.
10. Edge cases and risks to call out.

Read the actual files to ground your plan (models, schema.rb, config/routes.rb, the existing Api controller and jbuilder views, .devcontainer/, Gemfile, spec/rails_helper.rb). Return a thorough, well-structured plan I can hand to an implementer.

content.text:
I now have a complete picture. Here is the full implementation plan.

---

# Bidirectional Sync Implementation Plan — Crunchloop Rails Challenge Part 2

## 0. Context grounded in the actual repo

- `db/schema.rb` confirms `todo_lists` has **only** `name` (no `id`-aside timestamps), `todo_items` has `title`, `complete` (default false), `todo_list_id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`.
- Models at `app/models/todo_list.rb` / `todo_item.rb` are minimal; `TodoList has_many :todo_items, dependent: :destroy`.
- The existing API namespace pattern (`app/controllers/api/todo_lists_controller.rb`, jbuilder at `app/views/api/todo_lists/index.json.jbuilder`) shows the local app *exposes* an outbound API too — the external `source_id` field is designed to hold *our* local id when the external system pulls from us. This matters: when we POST to the external system, we set `source_id` = our local record id, which gives us a stable correlation key even before we learn the external `id`.
- `app/controllers/application_controller.rb` raises on unknown formats — unrelated to sync but confirms conventions.
- RSpec is configured with transactional fixtures (`spec/rails_helper.rb`); no `spec/support` autoload yet (the `Dir[...support...]` line is commented). We'll need to uncomment/add support wiring for WebMock.
- `Procfile.dev` exists (`web` + `css`) — we add a `worker` line.
- Devcontainer: Ruby 3.1, `postCreateCommand` runs `bundle install && rake db:setup`.

---

## 1. Schema / migration design

Four migrations, each small and reversible. Keep `external_id`/`source_id` as STRINGS (external IDs are strings).

### Migration A — add timestamps to `todo_lists` (currently missing)
```
add_timestamps :todo_lists, null: true   # backfill, then change to null: false
```
Two-step within one migration: `add_timestamps` with `null: true`, backfill existing rows to `current_timestamp`, then `change_column_null` to `false`. This is required because last-write-wins needs a local `updated_at` on lists, and lists currently have none. This is the single most important schema gap.

### Migration B — sync columns on `todo_lists`
- `external_id` (string, indexed, unique where not null) — the external system's `id`.
- `last_synced_at` (datetime, nullable) — when this record was last successfully reconciled.

### Migration C — sync columns on `todo_items`
- `external_id` (string, indexed, unique where not null).
- `last_synced_at` (datetime, nullable).

### Migration D — deletion tracking (the design decision)

**The problem:** a local hard delete (`dependent: :destroy`) leaves no row, so the next sync can't tell "deleted locally, must DELETE on external" apart from "never existed locally, must PULL-create from external." Without a trace, a delete you made locally would be *resurrected* by the next pull.

**Options considered:**

| Option | How | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soft delete (`deleted_at` column + default scope) | Records never physically removed; `deleted_at` set instead | Full history; simple to reason about; one place | Forces `default_scope`/`unscope` everywhere; changes existing app + existing specs (`destroys its items when destroyed` test); leaks sync concern into the whole domain |
| **Tombstone / sync-log table** | Separate `sync_tombstones(record_type, record_id, external_id, deleted_at)` written via model callbacks on destroy | Domain models untouched; existing destroy semantics & specs keep passing; explicit, sync-owned; easy to purge after propagation | One extra table + callback; must remember to write it |
| Do nothing | — | Trivial | Deletes get resurrected — correctness bug |

**Recommendation: the tombstone table.** It keeps the existing app and its passing specs (notably the `dependent: :destroy` test) completely intact, and isolates the sync concern. For a challenge submission this reads as a deliberate, well-justified design decision rather than an invasive `default_scope` refactor.

```
create_table :sync_tombstones do |t|
  t.string   :record_type, null: false   # "TodoList" | "TodoItem"
  t.bigint   :record_id,   null: false   # local id (for traceability)
  t.string   :external_id                # null if record was never pushed
  t.bigint   :parent_external_id_ref     # for items: the list's external id, needed for DELETE /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}
  t.datetime :deleted_at,  null: false
  t.datetime :propagated_at              # set once the external DELETE succeeds
  t.timestamps
end
add_index :sync_tombstones, [:record_type, :external_id]
add_index :sync_tombstones, :propagated_at
```

A tombstone is only actionable if it has an `external_id` (the record existed remotely). After a successful external DELETE we set `propagated_at` (idempotency — never delete twice). Old propagated tombstones can be purged periodically.

**Model wiring** (concern `app/models/concerns/syncable.rb` or `after_destroy` hooks): on destroy of a `TodoList`/`TodoItem` that has an `external_id`, create a tombstone capturing `external_id` (and for items, the parent list's `external_id`). For items destroyed via `dependent: :destroy` cascade when the *whole list* is deleted, we typically only need the list-level DELETE (external cascades), so we can skip per-item tombstones in that path to avoid noise — but we still need the item-level tombstone when a single item is deleted while its list survives.

---

## 2. Reconciliation algorithm

### Matching strategy (correlation keys, in priority order)
1. **`external_id`** on the local record ↔ external `id`. Primary key once a record has been synced.
2. **Fallback: `source_id`** — when we POST a new local record to the external system we set `source_id` = local id. So an external record whose `source_id` equals a local record's id is the same record even if we never persisted its `external_id` (e.g., the push succeeded but the local `external_id` write failed — a crash window). On pull we use this to repair the link instead of creating a duplicate.
3. No match either way → genuinely one-sided.

Build two indexes per sync run for O(1) matching:
- `local_by_external_id`, `local_by_id` (for source_id fallback).
- `external_by_id`, `external_by_source_id`.

### Decision matrix — TodoLists (top level)

Let `L` = local record, `R` = external (remote) record. `last-write-wins` compares `L.updated_at` vs `R.updated_at` (both parsed to UTC; external timestamps come as ISO strings).

| Case | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Local only, never pushed** | `L.external_id` nil, no `R` matches by source_id | PUSH create: `POST /todolists` with `source_id=L.id, name, items:[...]` (nested). Store returned `external_id` on `L` and on each item. Set `last_synced_at`. |
| **External only, no local** | `R` exists, no `L` by external_id and no `L.id == R.source_id` | PULL create: build local `TodoList` (+ nested items) with `external_id=R.id`. Set `last_synced_at`. |
| **Both exist** | matched | Compare timestamps → see update rules below; then recurse into items. |
| **Tombstone (local deleted)** | actionable tombstone with `external_id` and nil `propagated_at` | PUSH delete: `DELETE /todolists/{external_id}`; on success set `propagated_at`. |
| **External deleted** | `L.external_id` present + `L.last_synced_at` present, but `R` absent from the full GET | PULL delete: destroy `L` locally. (Guard: only when `L` was previously synced — a brand-new unsynced local list also has no `R`, but it's caught by the "local only, never pushed" case because its `external_id` is nil.) |

**Update rule (both exist):**
- If `L.updated_at > R.updated_at` and the mapped fields differ → PATCH external (`PATCH /todolists/{id}` body `{name}`).
- If `R.updated_at > L.updated_at` and fields differ → update `L` locally (`name`), **without bumping `updated_at` past R** (use `update_columns` or set `updated_at = R.updated_at` so we don't create a false "local is newer" on the next run).
- If equal or no field diff → no-op (dirty check minimizes calls).
- Always recurse into items.

### Decision matrix — TodoItems (nested within a matched list)

Match items within the list scope by `external_id`, fallback `source_id`. Field mapping: local `title` ↔ external `description`; local `complete` ↔ external `completed`.

| Case | Action |
|---|---|
| Local item, no external_id, list NOT yet on external | Handled by the parent's nested `POST` (items go in the create body). |
| Local item, no external_id, **list already exists on external** | **The API gap** (no item-create endpoint). See strategy below. |
| External item, no local match | PULL create local `TodoItem(external_id=R.id, title=R.description, complete=R.completed)`. |
| Both exist | Last-write-wins: if local newer & differs → `PATCH /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}` body `{description, completed}`; if external newer & differs → update local columns; else no-op. |
| Local item tombstone w/ external_id | `DELETE /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}`; set `propagated_at`. |
| External item missing but local was synced | PULL delete: destroy local item. |

### The "new item on an existing external list" gap

The external API only supports item creation nested in `POST /todolists`. Three strategies, with a recommendation:

1. **Recreate-list strategy:** DELETE the external list and re-POST it with all items. Correct but destructive — loses external item IDs/history and risks data loss if the external side had its own changes. Reject.
2. **Accept-as-limitation + document:** push new items only at list-creation time; for new items added to an already-synced list, log a warning and skip (or pull-only). Honest, low-risk.
3. **Recommended — "best effort with documented limitation":** Attempt nothing destructive. In `push` for an existing list, if there are local items with no `external_id`, log them clearly and record a non-fatal `SyncResult` warning. Document prominently in NOTES.md that the external contract lacks an item-create endpoint, so newly-created local items on an existing list cannot be pushed without a list recreate, which we deliberately avoid. This demonstrates you understood the contract constraint and made a safe call. (Mention recreate as the alternative if the product accepted destructive sync.)

### Last-write-wins precision note

External lists have `created_at/updated_at`; after Migration A, local lists do too. Compare in UTC. Define a tolerance: if the absolute difference is within a small epsilon (e.g., 1s) treat as equal/no-op to avoid ping-pong from clock skew and serialization rounding.

---

## 3. Performance — minimize external calls

- **One GET pulls everything:** `GET /todolists` returns all lists *with nested items*. A single call gives the entire remote snapshot — no per-list or per-item GETs.
- **Batch create via nested POST:** new local lists send all their items in one `POST` body.
- **Dirty checks before PATCH:** only PATCH when the mapped fields actually differ AND the side is the newer writer. Never blind-write.
- **In-memory diff:** build the two index hashes once, do all matching in Ruby; the only network calls are the single GET plus the necessary mutations.
- **Skip clean records:** records where `last_synced_at >= updated_at` on both sides and present in the snapshot need no write.
- Optional future optimization (note only): if the external API later supports `If-Modified-Since` or a `?since=` filter, switch from full-snapshot to delta pull.

---

## 4. Resilience

- **Partial failure isolation:** the reconciler processes records independently. A failure on one list/item is caught, recorded in a `SyncResult` (per-record status: created/updated/deleted/skipped/failed + error), and the loop continues. One bad record never aborts the run.
- **Per-record retry/backoff inside the service:** wrap each external mutation in a small retry helper (e.g., 3 attempts, exponential backoff 0.5s/1s/2s) for transient errors (timeouts, 5xx, connection reset). 4xx (except 429) are non-retryable and recorded as failures immediately.
- **Idempotency (re-run safety):**
  - `source_id` + `external_id` correlation means a re-run never double-creates: a record pushed but whose local `external_id` write was lost is re-matched by `source_id` on the next GET and the link is repaired rather than re-created.
  - Tombstones with `propagated_at` are never re-deleted.
  - `last_synced_at` + dirty checks make redundant PATCHes no-ops.
  - The whole run is convergent: running it twice with no changes performs zero mutations the second time.
- **Whole-job retry via ActiveJob:** `retry_on` for transient infrastructure failures (e.g., the external host briefly down) so the *job* retries; because the service is idempotent, a job retry safely re-reconciles only what's still pending. `discard_on` for permanent errors (e.g., malformed config).
- **Transaction boundaries:** wrap *local* writes for a single record (list + its items, or item) in a DB transaction so a local-side failure doesn't leave a half-linked record. Do NOT wrap the whole run in one transaction (you'd roll back already-confirmed external mutations).
- **Logging:** structured, tagged logger (`Rails.logger.tagged("Sync")`), one line per decision (`PUSH_CREATE list source_id=…`, `PATCH item external_id=… fields=[completed]`, `SKIP clean`, `FAIL … error=…`). Emit a summary at the end (counts per action + failures). Return the `SyncResult` so the rake task can print it and tests can assert on it.

---

## 5. File / module layout

```
app/
  services/
    external_todo_api/
      client.rb            # HTTP wrapper: list_todolists, create_todolist, update_todolist,
                           #   delete_todolist, update_todoitem, delete_todoitem
      configuration.rb     # base_url from ENV, timeouts, Faraday connection
      resource_error.rb    # raised on non-2xx, carries status + body; flags retryable?
  sync/
    todo_sync_service.rb   # PORO orchestrator: pull snapshot, run reconciler, apply, return SyncResult
    reconciler.rb          # pure-ish diff engine: given local state + remote snapshot -> list of Actions
    list_reconciler.rb     # per-list matching incl. nested item reconciliation (optional split)
    action.rb             # value object: {kind, target, payload}
    sync_result.rb         # accumulates per-record outcomes + summary
    mapper.rb              # field mapping local<->external (title<->description, complete<->completed)
  models/
    concerns/
      syncable.rb          # after_destroy -> create tombstone (when external_id present)
    sync_tombstone.rb      # tombstone model
  jobs/
    todo_sync_job.rb       # ActiveJob: retry_on/discard_on, calls TodoSyncService.new(...).call

lib/
  tasks/
    sync.rake              # `rake sync:run` (the runnable POC) -> TodoSyncService.new.call; prints result

config/
  initializers/
    external_todo_api.rb   # reads ENV["EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL"] (default http://localhost:3001 or similar)
  # queue_adapter set to solid_queue in config/environments/*

spec/
  support/webmock.rb       # require 'webmock/rspec'; WebMock.disable_net_connect!
  services/external_todo_api/client_spec.rb
  sync/reconciler_spec.rb
  sync/todo_sync_service_spec.rb        # integration-style with WebMock
  jobs/todo_sync_job_spec.rb
  models/sync_tombstone_spec.rb
  fixtures/ or spec/support/api_responses/  # canned JSON for stubs
```

**Separation that makes it testable:** `Reconciler` is pure — it takes the local records and the parsed remote snapshot and returns a list of `Action`s with **no I/O**. `TodoSyncService` does the I/O (calls `Client.list_todolists`, runs the reconciler, applies actions through the client + ActiveRecord). This lets you unit-test the entire decision matrix without HTTP at all, and reserve WebMock for the client and the integration test.

**Config:** `EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL` env var, read in an initializer into `ExternalTodoApi::Configuration`. The client reads from config so tests can point it at a stub host.

---

## 6. Rails 7.0 → 7.1 + Solid Queue (minimal, low-risk)

### Rails upgrade
1. Gemfile: `gem "rails", "~> 7.1.0"` (and bump `puma` to `~> 6.0`, which 7.1 apps expect; 5.x works but 6 is cleaner). `bundle update rails`.
2. Run `bin/rails app:update` — review/accept new framework defaults files; **do not** blindly flip `config.load_defaults 7.1` until verified. Safer path: keep `load_defaults 7.0`, then create `config/initializers/new_framework_defaults_7_1.rb` and opt into changes incrementally. For a clean submission, flipping to `7.1` and running the suite is fine since the app is tiny — but call out the deprecations you resolved in NOTES.md.
3. The stdlib gem shims currently pinned in the Gemfile (`logger`, `base64`, `bigdecimal`, `mutex_m`, `drb`, `benchmark`, `ostruct`, `cgi`) were added for Ruby/Rails 7.0 compatibility; with 7.1 + Ruby 3.1 most are still harmless to keep. Leave them to minimize risk.
4. `bundle exec rspec` — fix any deprecations. Regenerate `db/schema.rb` header to `[7.1]` via a migration run.

### Solid Queue install
1. Gemfile: `gem "solid_queue"`. `bundle install`.
2. `bin/rails solid_queue:install` (generates the migration for the queue tables + a `config/queue.yml` or initializer depending on version). On 7.1, Solid Queue runs on its own tables in the **same SQLite DB** for the challenge (a separate `queue` database via multi-db is the production-grade option — note it but keep single-DB for simplicity).
3. Run the generated migration: adds `solid_queue_*` tables.
4. Config: in `config/environments/development.rb` and `production.rb`: `config.active_job.queue_adapter = :solid_queue`. For **test**: `config.active_job.queue_adapter = :test` (so `TodoSyncJob` enqueues can be asserted without a running worker).
5. Worker process: add to `Procfile.dev`:
   ```
   worker: bin/rails solid_queue:start
   ```
   (or `bin/jobs` on newer Solid Queue). Document running it for the queued path; the rake task path needs no worker.
6. Devcontainer: no change strictly required since `postCreateCommand` runs `bundle install && rake db:setup`, and `db:setup` will load the new schema including queue tables. Optionally note that the worker must be started manually (`bin/rails solid_queue:start`) or add it to a documented startup step. Keep `postCreateCommand` as-is to stay low-risk.

---

## 7. HTTP client: Faraday vs Net::HTTP

**Recommendation: Faraday.** Rationale for a clean challenge submission:
- Clean, declarative connection setup (base_url, JSON request/response middleware, timeouts) — less boilerplate than hand-rolling `Net::HTTP` request objects and JSON parsing.
- First-class with WebMock and VCR (the testing requirement), which everyone recognizes.
- Pluggable retry middleware (`faraday-retry`) complements the in-service retry, and easy error-handling via `response.success?` / `raise_error` middleware.
- It's the de-facto standard for Ruby API clients, so reviewers read it instantly.

Trade-off: one extra dependency vs. zero-dep `Net::HTTP`. For a *library-grade* sync client the readability win outweighs the dependency. Add `gem "faraday"` (and optionally `gem "faraday-retry"`). If you wanted zero new runtime deps, `Net::HTTP` + `JSON` is viable and I'd wrap it behind the same `Client` interface — but Faraday is the better submission choice.

---

## 8. Test plan

### Reconciler unit tests (`spec/sync/reconciler_spec.rb`) — pure, no HTTP
Feed in local records (built/created) + a parsed remote snapshot hash, assert the returned `Action` list. Cover the full matrix:
- local-only list → expect a `push_create` action with nested items + `source_id`.
- external-only list → expect `pull_create` with mapped fields.
- both, local newer & different → `push_update`.
- both, external newer & different → `pull_update`.
- both, equal/no diff → no action (clean skip).
- tombstone with external_id → `push_delete`.
- previously-synced local list absent from snapshot → `pull_delete`.
- item-level variants of each within a matched list (incl. field mapping `title↔description`, `complete↔completed`).
- the API-gap case: new item on existing external list → expect a warning action / skip, not a destructive recreate.
- timestamp tie within epsilon → no action.
- `source_id` fallback: external record whose `source_id` == local id but local `external_id` nil → expect link-repair, not duplicate create.

### Client tests with WebMock (`spec/services/external_todo_api/client_spec.rb`)
- `list_todolists` → stub `GET /todolists` returning canned nested JSON; assert parsed structure.
- `create_todolist` → assert request body contains `source_id`, `name`, nested `items` with `description`/`completed`; stub 201 with returned `id`s; assert client returns them.
- `update_todolist` / `update_todoitem` → assert PATCH path + body shape.
- `delete_*` → assert DELETE path; assert 204 handled.
- Error handling: stub 500 → raises retryable `ResourceError`; stub 422 → raises non-retryable; stub timeout (`to_timeout`) → retryable. Assert `disable_net_connect!` is active (no real calls leak).

### Integration-style sync test (`spec/sync/todo_sync_service_spec.rb`)
- Seed local DB + stub the full external surface with WebMock. Run `TodoSyncService.new.call`.
- Assert: correct external requests were made (WebMock `have_requested` with body matchers), local DB ends in the expected converged state (new pulled lists/items exist with `external_id` set, `last_synced_at` set, tombstones marked `propagated_at`).
- **Idempotency assertion:** run `.call` a second time with the snapshot updated to reflect the first run; assert **zero** mutating requests the second time.
- **Partial-failure assertion:** stub one PATCH to 500; assert the run completes, that record is marked failed in `SyncResult`, and the other records still synced.

### Job test (`spec/jobs/todo_sync_job_spec.rb`)
- With `:test` adapter, assert `TodoSyncJob.perform_later` enqueues; `perform_now` invokes `TodoSyncService`. Assert `retry_on` is configured (e.g., it re-enqueues on the transient error class). Keep this thin — the heavy logic is tested in the service.

### Tombstone model test
- Destroying a synced list/item creates a tombstone with `external_id`; destroying an unsynced record does not.

### Test infra
- Add `gem "webmock"` (and optionally `vcr`) to `:test`. Recommend **WebMock** primarily (explicit, readable stubs); VCR optional for the integration test if you want recorded cassettes — but WebMock alone satisfies the requirement and keeps the repo free of cassette files. Create `spec/support/webmock.rb` and uncomment the `spec/support` autoload line in `spec/rails_helper.rb`.

---

## 9. NOTES.md outline

1. **Overview** — what the sync does (bidirectional, last-write-wins).
2. **How to run** — `EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL=... bundle exec rake sync:run` (POC); queued path via `TodoSyncJob.perform_later` + starting the Solid Queue worker.
3. **Architecture** — Client / Reconciler (pure) / Service / Job / Rake separation and why.
4. **Data model & migrations** — new columns, the tombstone table, and the timestamps-added-to-todo_lists fix.
5. **Reconciliation algorithm** — matching keys (external_id → source_id fallback), the decision matrix, last-write-wins with epsilon.
6. **Field mapping** — `title↔description`, `complete↔completed`, `source_id` semantics.
7. **Resilience & idempotency** — per-record retry, ActiveJob retry, re-run safety, transactions, logging.
8. **Performance** — single-GET snapshot, nested batch create, dirty-check PATCH.
9. **Known limitations / trade-offs** — the **item-create API gap** (no endpoint to add an item to an existing external list; recreate avoided deliberately); single SQLite DB shared with Solid Queue; full-snapshot pull (no delta); last-write-wins drops the losing edit (no field-level merge).
10. **Decisions log** — tombstone vs soft-delete, Faraday vs Net::HTTP, Rails 7.1 + Solid Queue.
11. **Testing** — how the suite is structured and how to run it.
12. **Future work** — delta sync, separate queue DB, conflict audit trail, webhook-driven sync.

---

## 10. Edge cases & risks

- **`todo_lists` has no timestamps today** — last-write-wins is impossible for lists until Migration A lands. Highest-priority fix; backfill carefully so existing rows don't all look "ancient."
- **Crash between external POST and local `external_id` write** — handled by `source_id` fallback on re-run (link repair, no duplicate).
- **Delete resurrection** — without tombstones a local delete is undone by the next pull; tombstones + `propagated_at` prevent this and prevent double-deletes.
- **Clock skew / timestamp rounding** between systems → ping-pong updates. Mitigated by the epsilon tolerance and by setting local `updated_at` to the remote value on pull-update.
- **External `description`/`completed` nil or type surprises** (the spec was ambiguous on item field types) — mapper must coerce `completed` to boolean and validate `description` presence to satisfy the local `title` presence validation; record a failure rather than crash if invalid.
- **Validation failures on pull-create** (e.g., empty external name) — wrap in transaction, record failure, continue.
- **Item-create gap** — new local items on an existing external list can't be pushed without a destructive recreate; documented as a limitation.
- **`dependent: :destroy` cascade vs item tombstones** — deleting a whole list shouldn't spam per-item tombstones (external cascades on list DELETE); only single-item deletes need item tombstones. Get this guard right or you'll issue DELETEs for items the list-delete already removed.
- **No auth / no pagination in the external API** — fine for the challenge; note that a real system needs both (and pagination would break the single-GET assumption).
- **Rails 7.1 `load_defaults` flip** could surface deprecations in the existing app; verify the full suite, and the existing `Api::TodoListsController` format-error behavior, still pass.
- **Solid Queue on SQLite** — works for the POC but has concurrency limits; acceptable, noted as a trade-off.
- **Concurrent sync runs** — two overlapping runs could double-act. Add a simple guard (advisory lock / a "sync in progress" flag) or document that runs are expected to be serialized by the single worker.

### Critical Files for Implementation
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/db/schema.rb (and new files under /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/db/migrate/)
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/app/models/todo_list.rb and /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/app/models/todo_item.rb
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/Gemfile
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/spec/rails_helper.rb
- /Users/rootstrap/Codes/crunchloop-rails-interview/config/application.rb (and config/environments/* for the Solid Queue adapter)

Note on process: I was given conflicting instructions (a read-only planning role with no edit tools, vs. plan mode offering a plan file). I treated this as read-only and returned the full plan here rather than writing any file. If you'd like, I can hand this off to an implementer or you can switch me out of plan mode to execute it.

input.content:
# Plan — Parte 2: Sistema de sincronización (Crunchloop challenge)

## Contexto

El challenge "senior engineer" (Parte 2) pide construir un mecanismo de **sincronización bidireccional**
entre la app Rails de la entrevista (fuente local, SQLite) y una **API externa REST** de Todos
(sin auth, base URL configurable). La consigna exige soportar **create / update / delete en ambas
direcciones** ("detect changes in external API" + "propagate local modifications"), con **resiliencia**
(fallos parciales + reintentos), **performance** (minimizar llamadas a la API externa) y **logging/errores
claros**. Entregables: código de sync + tests, un **POC ejecutable**, un **`NOTES.md`** de decisiones de
diseño, y que **todo corra en dev containers**.

Dato de diseño central del OpenAPI externo (`docs/external-api.yaml`): los modelos externos tienen `id`
(string) **y** `source_id` (string). `source_id` está pensado para guardar el id del sistema de origen
(el nuestro), lo que da una clave de correlación estable entre ambos lados.

### Decisiones confirmadas con el usuario
1. **Alcance**: sincronización **bidireccional / de reconciliación**. Push local→externa y pull
   externa→local (create/update/delete en ambos sentidos, incluyendo crear registros locales cuando
   aparecen en la externa). Conflictos: **last-write-wins** por `updated_at`.
2. **Testing**: **mockear el HTTP externo** (WebMock). Base URL por ENV; no depender de un servicio vivo.
3. **Ejecución**: **upgrade Rails 7.0.4.3 → 7.1.x + Solid Queue**. El core del sync es un **PORO**
   testeable, invocable por un **rake task (POC)** y por un **ActiveJob** encolado en Solid Queue.
   Reintentos: `retry_on` de ActiveJob para fallos transitorios del job + retry/backoff por-registro
   dentro del service para no rehacer lo ya sincronizado.

### Mapeo de campos local ↔ externo
- `TodoList`: local `name` ↔ externo `name`.
- `TodoItem`: local `title` ↔ externo `description`; local `complete` ↔ externo `completed`.
- IDs externos son **strings**.

---

## Cambios de schema (4 migraciones, chicas y reversibles)

1. **Timestamps en `todo_lists`** (¡hoy no tiene!). `add_timestamps :todo_lists, null: true` → backfill de
   filas existentes a `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` → `change_column_null` a `false`. Sin esto no hay last-write-wins
   para listas.
2. **Sync columns en `todo_lists`**: `external_id` (string, índice único where not null), `last_synced_at`
   (datetime, nullable).
3. **Sync columns en `todo_items`**: `external_id` (string, índice único where not null), `last_synced_at`.
4. **Tombstones de borrado** — tabla `sync_tombstones`:
   `record_type` ("TodoList"|"TodoItem"), `record_id` (bigint local), `external_id` (string, nil si nunca
   se pusheó), `parent_external_id` (string, para el path de delete de items), `deleted_at`,
   `propagated_at` (se setea cuando el DELETE externo tuvo éxito → idempotencia). Índices en
   `[record_type, external_id]` y `propagated_at`.

**Por qué tombstones y no soft-delete**: un hard delete local (`dependent: :destroy`) no deja rastro, y el
pull lo "resucitaría". El tombstone aísla la preocupación de sync **sin** meter `default_scope` en el
dominio ni romper specs existentes (p.ej. el test de `dependent: :destroy`). Es la opción menos invasiva y
más explícita para una entrega de challenge.

**Wiring**: concern `Syncable` (`after_destroy`) crea el tombstone **solo si el registro tenía
`external_id`**. Al borrar una lista completa el DELETE externo cascadea sus items, así que **no** se
generan tombstones por-item en ese path (solo cuando se borra un item individual cuya lista sobrevive).

---

## Algoritmo de reconciliación

**Matching** (en orden de prioridad): (1) `external_id` local ↔ `id` externo; (2) fallback por
`source_id` (externo cuyo `source_id == local.id` pero `external_id` local quedó nil por un crash entre el
POST y el guardado → **repara el link**, no duplica); (3) sin match → un solo lado.
Se construyen índices en memoria por lado para matching O(1) y se hace todo el diff en Ruby.

**Matriz de decisión — TodoLists** (L=local, R=remoto; comparar `updated_at` en UTC con epsilon ~1s):

| Caso | Acción |
|---|---|
| Local sin `external_id`, sin R por source_id | **Push create**: `POST /todolists` con `source_id=L.id, name, items:[...]` (anidado). Guardar `external_id` en lista e items + `last_synced_at`. |
| R sin L (y `L.id != R.source_id`) | **Pull create**: crear `TodoList` local + items con `external_id=R.id`. |
| Ambos, L más nuevo y difiere | **Push update**: `PATCH /todolists/{id}` body `{name}`. |
| Ambos, R más nuevo y difiere | **Pull update** local; setear `updated_at = R.updated_at` (evitar falso "local newer"). |
| Ambos, iguales / sin diff | No-op (dirty check). |
| Tombstone con `external_id`, sin `propagated_at` | **Push delete**: `DELETE /todolists/{external_id}`; setear `propagated_at`. |
| L previamente sincronizado (`external_id` + `last_synced_at`) ausente del GET | **Pull delete**: destruir L local. |

**TodoItems** (anidados en la lista ya matcheada): mismo patrón, con mapeo `title↔description`,
`complete↔completed`. Update por last-write-wins → `PATCH /todolists/{listId}/todoitems/{itemId}` body
`{description, completed}` o update local. Delete vía tombstone → `DELETE .../todoitems/{itemId}`.

**Gap de la API**: no hay endpoint para crear un item en una lista externa **ya existente** (solo anidado
en el POST de la lista). Estrategia elegida: **no hacer nada destructivo**. Loguear warning + registrar
warning no-fatal en `SyncResult`; **documentar la limitación en NOTES.md** (recrear la lista se descarta
por riesgo de pérdida de datos). Demuestra que se entendió el contrato.

---

## Performance
- **Un solo `GET /todolists`** trae todo el snapshot remoto con items anidados (sin GETs por lista/item).
- **Batch create** vía POST anidado (lista + todos sus items en una llamada).
- **Dirty check antes de PATCH**: solo escribir cuando los campos mapeados difieren y ese lado es el más
  nuevo. Nunca escritura ciega.
- Saltear registros limpios (`last_synced_at >= updated_at` y presentes en el snapshot).

## Resiliencia
- **Aislamiento de fallos parciales**: el reconciler procesa cada registro de forma independiente; un fallo
  se captura en `SyncResult` (status por-registro + error) y el loop continúa.
- **Retry/backoff por-registro** dentro del service (p.ej. 3 intentos, backoff 0.5/1/2s) para errores
  transitorios (timeout, 5xx, 429). 4xx (salvo 429) = fallo no reintentable inmediato.
- **Idempotencia / re-run safe**: correlación por `source_id`/`external_id` evita doble-create;
  `propagated_at` evita doble-delete; dirty checks + `last_synced_at` hacen no-op las escrituras
  redundantes. Una segunda corrida sin cambios ejecuta **cero** mutaciones.
- **Retry del job** vía `retry_on` (infra transitoria) y `discard_on` (errores permanentes).
- **Transacciones** por-registro (lista+items, o item) en el lado local; **no** envolver toda la corrida.
- **Logging** estructurado con `Rails.logger.tagged("Sync")`: una línea por decisión + resumen final
  (conteos por acción + fallos). El service devuelve `SyncResult` para el rake task y los tests.

---

## Layout de archivos

```
app/services/external_todo_api/
  client.rb           # Faraday wrapper: list_todolists, create/update/delete_todolist, update/delete_todoitem
  configuration.rb    # base_url desde ENV, timeouts
  resource_error.rb   # error con status+body; flag retryable?
app/sync/
  todo_sync_service.rb # PORO orquestador: pull snapshot -> reconciler -> aplica -> SyncResult
  reconciler.rb        # motor de diff PURO (sin I/O): estado local + snapshot remoto -> [Action]
  action.rb            # value object {kind, target, payload}
  sync_result.rb       # acumula resultados por-registro + resumen
  mapper.rb            # mapeo de campos local<->externo
app/models/concerns/syncable.rb   # after_destroy -> tombstone
app/models/sync_tombstone.rb
app/jobs/todo_sync_job.rb         # retry_on/discard_on -> TodoSyncService.call
lib/tasks/sync.rake               # `rake sync:run` (POC) -> imprime SyncResult
config/initializers/external_todo_api.rb  # ENV["EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL"]
```

**Clave de testeo**: `Reconciler` es puro (recibe registros locales + snapshot parseado, devuelve `Action`s,
sin HTTP) → toda la matriz se testea sin red. `TodoSyncService` hace el I/O. WebMock solo para el client y
el test de integración.

**HTTP client**: **Faraday** (+ opcional `faraday-retry`) — setup declarativo, middleware JSON, integración
de primera con WebMock/VCR, estándar reconocible. (Net::HTTP sería la alternativa zero-dep tras la misma
interfaz `Client`.)

---

## Upgrade Rails 7.1 + Solid Queue (mínimo y de bajo riesgo)
1. Gemfile: `gem "rails", "~> 7.1.0"` (subir `puma` a `~> 6.0`). `bundle update rails`.
2. `bin/rails app:update` — revisar archivos de defaults; flipear `config.load_defaults 7.1` y correr la
   suite (la app es chica). Documentar deprecaciones resueltas en NOTES.md.
3. `gem "solid_queue"` → `bin/rails solid_queue:install` → correr migración (tablas `solid_queue_*` en la
   misma SQLite; multi-db queda como nota de prod).
4. Adapter: `config.active_job.queue_adapter = :solid_queue` en development/production; `:test` en test
   (para asertar enqueues sin worker).
5. Worker: agregar `worker: bin/rails solid_queue:start` al `Procfile.dev`.
6. Devcontainer: `postCreateCommand` (`bundle install && rake db:setup`) ya carga el schema nuevo;
   documentar arranque del worker. Sin cambios obligatorios al devcontainer.

---

## Plan de tests (RSpec + WebMock)
- `spec/support/webmock.rb`: `require 'webmock/rspec'` + `WebMock.disable_net_connect!`; descomentar el
  autoload de `spec/support` en `rails_helper.rb`. Agregar `gem "webmock"` a `:test`.
- **Reconciler (puro)** — toda la matriz: local-only→push_create (con items + source_id),
  external-only→pull_create, ambos L-newer→push_update, ambos R-newer→pull_update, iguales→no-op,
  tombstone→push_delete, ausente-previamente-sincronizado→pull_delete, variantes a nivel item (con mapeo de
  campos), gap de item nuevo→warning/skip, empate dentro del epsilon→no-op, fallback por source_id→repara
  link (no duplica).
- **Client (WebMock)**: cada endpoint; asertar path + shape del body (`source_id`, `description`,
  `completed`); manejo de 500 (retryable), 422 (no-retryable), timeout (retryable); `disable_net_connect!`.
- **Integración (`TodoSyncService`)**: seed local + stub del surface externo → `.call`; asertar requests
  (`have_requested`) y estado local convergido (`external_id`/`last_synced_at` seteados, tombstones
  `propagated_at`). **Idempotencia**: segunda corrida → cero mutaciones. **Fallo parcial**: un PATCH a 500 →
  la corrida termina, ese registro queda failed en `SyncResult`, el resto sincroniza.
- **Job**: con adapter `:test`, `perform_later` encola; `perform_now` invoca el service; `retry_on`
  configurado. **Tombstone model**: destruir registro sincronizado crea tombstone con `external_id`;
  destruir uno no-sincronizado, no.

---

## NOTES.md (secciones)
Overview · Cómo correr (`EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL=... bundle exec rake sync:run` + path encolado con worker) ·
Arquitectura (Client/Reconciler puro/Service/Job/Rake) · Modelo de datos y migraciones (incl. fix de
timestamps en todo_lists) · Algoritmo de reconciliación (matching, matriz, last-write-wins + epsilon) ·
Mapeo de campos y semántica de `source_id` · Resiliencia e idempotencia · Performance · Limitaciones y
trade-offs (gap de item-create, SQLite compartida con Solid Queue, pull full-snapshot, LWW descarta la
edición perdedora) · Log de decisiones (tombstone vs soft-delete, Faraday vs Net::HTTP, Rails 7.1 + Solid
Queue) · Testing · Trabajo futuro (delta sync, queue DB separada, audit de conflictos, webhooks).

---

## Edge cases / riesgos a vigilar
- `todo_lists` sin timestamps hoy (fix prioritario; backfill cuidadoso).
- Crash entre POST externo y guardado de `external_id` → reparado por fallback `source_id`.
- Resurrección de borrados → tombstones + `propagated_at`.
- Clock skew / redondeo de timestamps → epsilon + setear `updated_at` local al valor remoto en pull-update.
- Tipos sorpresa en campos externos (`description`/`completed` nil) → mapper coerce a boolean / valida
  presencia; registrar failure en vez de crashear.
- Cascade `dependent: :destroy` vs tombstones por-item (no spamear DELETEs de items que el delete de la
  lista ya removió).
- Flip de `load_defaults 7.1` puede traer deprecaciones → verificar suite completa.
- Solid Queue sobre SQLite (límites de concurrencia, aceptable para el POC).
- Corridas concurrentes de sync → guard simple ("sync in progress") o documentar serialización por el worker.

---

## Verificación end-to-end
1. `bundle install && bin/rails db:migrate` (corre las 4 migraciones + las de Solid Queue).
2. `bundle exec rspec` — toda la suite verde (reconciler, client, integración, job, tombstone, + specs
   existentes tras el upgrade).
3. POC: `EXTERNAL_TODO_API_URL=http://localhost:xxxx bundle exec rake sync:run` → imprime el `SyncResult`
   (conteos por acción + fallos). Re-ejecutar → muestra cero mutaciones (idempotencia).
4. Path encolado: levantar worker (`bin/rails solid_queue:start`) y `TodoSyncJob.perform_later` desde la
   consola; verificar logs `[Sync]`.
5. Confirmar que todo levanta en el dev container (`bundle install && rake db:setup` en postCreate).
